coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of new gas diffusion electrode based on carbon quantum dot and its application for oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Kakaei, Karim
Javan, Hakimeh
Khamforoush, Mehrdad
Zarei, Seyed Amir
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared by one-pot electrochemical techniques cyclic voltammetry (CV) and AC impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at different potentials using graphite rods in ethanol alkaline electrolyte. The electrochemically synthesized CQD is carefully characterized by scanning electron microscopy, Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Ultra violet absorption, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, Raman spectrum, Chemiluminescence emission and electrochemical techniques. The morphology and microstructure methods confirm the formation of high quality CQD. The EIS of graphite electrode in NaOH/EtOH confirms the corrosion loop at low frequencies and as potential increases the charge transfer resistance decreases. Finally, we design a new gas diffusion electrode based on CQD pasted on carbon paper for the oxygen reduction reaction in fuel cells and compared it with commercially PtC catalysts using cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry and chronoamperometry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical synthesis
Graphite corrosion
Carbon quantum dot
Oxygen reduction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: EIS investigation of graphite corrosion in alkaline media
Electrochemical behavior of ORR on CQD

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2016 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD spectra of graphite and CQDs, (b) The inset represents enlarged CQD.
(a) SEM image of CQDs, (b) the inset is a HRTEM image of CQDs.
CL emission of the CQDs at different potentials.

CV curves of graphite electrode in pure ethanol and NaOH solution at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and different rang of potentials and the cycles saved at 1–200 scans, (a) CV at 0–1 V, (b) CV at 0–2 V, (c) CV at 0–3 V, (d) CV at 0–4 V, (e) CV at 0–5 V.
EIS Nyquist plots for graphite rod corrosion at different voltages (0.1–0.9 V) in ethanol solution and its equivalent circuit.
Illustrates the influence of increasing potential from (a) 1 V, (b) 2 V and (c) 3 V on the impedance response of graphite electrode in NaOH/ethanol solution at room temperate.
CVs of (a) bare GCE in saturated N2 (b) bare GCE in saturated O2 (c) CQD on GCE in saturated O2 at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 and 0.5 M KOH solution after 50th scan and (d) after 3000th scan.

(a) LSV of GDEs at 30 °C and in O2-saturated 0.5 M KOH with scan rate of 5 mV/s−1 (b) Tafel plots for GDEs extracted from (b). The loading of catalysts is 0.5 mg cm−2 for CQD and 30 μgPtcm−2 for commercial PtC on CP.
(a) Chronoamperometric plots for oxygen reduction at the GDE1 and GDE2 with PtC (b) Plots of I vs.−1/2 for oxygen reduction at GDEs at 300 K and an oxygen pressure of 1 atm.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of new gas diffusion electrode based on carbon quantum dot and its application for oxygen reduction reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry and Nano Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Maragheh, P.O. Box. 5181-83111, Maragheh, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physical Chemistry and Nano Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, University of Kurdistan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Sanandaj Branch, Islamic Azad University, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are prepared by one-pot electrochemical techniques cyclic voltammetry (CV) and AC impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at different potentials using graphite rods in ethanol alkaline electrolyte. The electrochemically synthesized CQD is carefully characterized by scanning electron microscopy, Transmission electron microscopy, X-ray diffraction, Ultra violet absorption, Fourier transform infrared spectrometry, Raman spectrum, Chemiluminescence emission and electrochemical techniques. The morphology and microstructure methods confirm the formation of high quality CQD. The EIS of graphite electrode in NaOH/EtOH confirms the corrosion loop at low frequencies and as potential increases the charge transfer resistance decreases. Finally, we design a new gas diffusion electrode based on CQD pasted on carbon paper for the oxygen reduction reaction in fuel cells and compared it with commercially PtC catalysts using cyclic voltammetry, linear sweep voltammetry and chronoamperometry.

ce:section.ce:para: The key components of polymer electrolyte fuel cells (PEFCs) are oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). In PEFCs, however numerous functional components work in order to produce electricity from the fuel and oxygen, the majority of the cost and durability arise from the cathode, where the electrochemical ORR occurs [1,2]. Platinum or other expensive constituents remain the most efficient ORR electro-catalysts, though the high cost and scarcity of platinum hinder further development of PEFC technologies based on these sources. In recent year, scientists have concentrated on developing relatively cheap and efficient ORR electro-catalyst with Pt-grade activity such as N- or halogen-carbon based material [2–5].

Carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes, carbon nanotubes and graphene, have generated much excitement for a wide variety of promising applications in nanotechnology, biosensing, and mobile energy [6–8]. Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new class of carbon nanomaterials, have great attention because of their interesting properties, such as non-blinking, water solubility, optical characteristics and nontoxicity according to currently available cytotoxic and in vivo toxic evaluation results [9,10].

It is known that the surface area and surface functional groups of carbon materials, especially the oxygen-containing groups, play a key role in improving the activities of methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) and ORR [11,12]. Therefore CQD and graphene quantum dot (GQD), as oxygen group enriched materials, are used in fuel cell with and without metal electro-catalysts [13,14].

Carbon nanostructures are usually prepared by laser ablation of graphite [15], thermal oxidation of suitable molecular precursors [16], microwave/ultrasonic passivation [17,18] and plasma treatment [19]. All these are efficient approaches to fabrication of CQDs. But, in most cases, the CQDs were produced with expensive carbon sources such as citric acid [20], glycerol [21], coffee grounds [22], soy milk [23], grass [24], bovine serum albumin [25] and egg [19].

However, electrochemical method is an effective tool to modify electronic states through adjusting the external power source to vary the Fermi energy level of electrode material surface from low-cost source such as graphite. In recent years, some researchers have attempted to produce CQDs by using electrochemical oxidation [26–28].

Here, we report for the first time, to the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of CQDs by using graphite and NaOH/EtOH as reactants by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Moreover, we can obtain information about the graphite exfoliation during the corrosion phenomena by EIS rout. The electrochemically synthesized CQD has been characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), ultra violet absorption (UV–Vis), FT-IR, raman and chemiluminescence (CL) emission. Finally, we report the high performance metal-free electro-catalyst for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) in alkaline media by CQDs as a gas diffusion electrode (GDE) with CV, linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and chronoamperometry (CA).

The electrochemical preparation of CQDs was carried out in a tree-electrode configuration. The electrolyte of the electrochemical process was organized by mixing ethanol/H2O (100 mL; volume ratio = 99.5:0.5) with a suitable amount (0.2–0.5 g) of NaOH, by using graphite rods as working electrode. We synthesized CQDs by CV [29] and EIS techniques in several potentials. A high-purity graphite rod was used as working electrode.

A mixture containing a homogeneous suspension of CQDs, Nafion solution and water were homogenized by sonication for 20 min, and then painted on to glassy carbon electrode. The obtained composite was dried at 100 °C for 1 h and denoted as (CQD/GC) electrode.

The preparation of the GDEs described previously [30]. But we shall briefly repeat the main features here. The ORR was studied at the GDE (geometric exposed area of 1 cm2) in 0.5 M potassium hydroxide (Merck) and constant temperature. The GDEs were fixed onto a Teflon holder containing a high pyrolytic graphite disk and oxygen purged from the back of the electrode. The electrochemical cell was connected to a potentiostat–galvanostat (VSP-300) for I–V polarization measurements, CA and also to a frequency response. The LSV measurement was recorded at 5 mV−1.

XRD analysis was carried out to analysis the structure of the CQDs and pristine graphite (obtained by grinding a graphite rod) as the comparison. The XRD patterns of the CQDs and pristine graphite are shown in Fig. 1 a, b. The spectrum of the graphite reflects one prominent peak at 26.49°, corresponding to the (002) planes of graphite, while for the pattern of CQDs there is one superimposed reflection at around 22–29°, suggesting the interlayer spacing of (002) diffraction peak is 0.39 nm. The larger interlayer spacing than that of graphite (0.34 nm) is ascribed to the existence of more functional groups [31]. This peak (d 002 = 3.9°A) is attributed to highly disordered carbon in CQDs. The decrease in the peak intensity and the increase of the full width half maximum (FWHM) are due to the small size of the resultant CQDs [32]. The crystallite size was estimated using the Scherer equation for the (002) peak, yielding value of 3.5 nm for CQDs.

Further characterization studies provided convincing evidence for the graphite fragment structure of the as synthesized CQDs. Typical ultra violet absorption (UV/Vis) spectrum of CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The absorption spectrum exhibits two peaks around 250 and 350 nm. The peak at 250 nm represents the aromatic π system absorption, which is similar to that of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (π–π* transition) [33]. While a peak at 350 nm is attributed to a n–π* transition [34].

Fig. 3 displays the Raman spectroscopes for CQDs. The well-known characteristics of CQDs in Raman spectra are the G band (∼1524 cm−1), which is generally assigned to the E2g phonon of sp2 bonds of carbon atoms, and the D band (∼1130 cm−1) as a breathing mode of k-point phonons of A1g symmetry [35], which is attributed to local defects and disorders. Hence, the smaller ID/IG peak intensity ratio of a Raman spectrum can indicate lower defects and disorders of the structures [35]. The ID/IG ratio is about 0.73.

Fig. 4 reveals the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of the as-prepared CQDs. The characteristic absorption bands of OH− at 3447 cm−1, the stretching vibration band of CO in COOH at 1651 cm−1, and the stretching vibration bands of CO at 1088 and 880 cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic acid and other oxygen-containing functional groups at their surfaces. Moreover, three obvious absorption peaks at 3000, 1410 and 1340 cm−1 are associated with the stretch of vibration CH, CC and CC, suggesting the presence of alkyl and aryl groups [31,36]. The FT-IR results are consistent with the XRD and Raman spectrum results. Thus, FT-IR measurement can be used to indicate the surface structure of CQDs.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image of the pure CQDs layer prepared by a electrochemical technique is shown in Fig. 5 a. This Fig. shows rough and full of spherical nanoparticles of graphite. Also transmission electron microscopy (TEM) has been used extensively as a powerful tool in the study of nanoscale materials, from which the size, morphology and crystalline lattices may be identified. TEM image of spherical nanoparticle reveals its graphitic layer structure (Fig. 5b). Fig. 5c shows that the carbon dots are monodispersed and homogeneous, with an average diameter of about 4 nm.

Fig. 6 shows the chemiluminescence (CL) emission of the CQDs that was prepared at various potentials. As shown from this Fig. the CQDs solution which is synthesized with lowers voltage possess lower CL intensity. On the other hand, by increasing the voltage in the CQDs preparation from 1 to 5 V, the CL intensity starts to increase and remain stable, which suggests that the CQDs concentration and production increase in the high potentials.

In order to investigate the structure of the CQDs that was built in pure ethanol and NaOH solution, the effect of various potentials from 1 to 5 V have been investigated using a Potentiostat–Galvanostat device and finally higher voltages have been applied using a DC-Power device which is demonstrated in Fig. 7 a–e. Fig. 7a and b show CVs of graphite electrode in ethanol and NaOH solution at 0–1 V and 0–2 V. The NaOH electrolysis (due to small amount) slowly takes place, which could be related to OH− oxidation. It is clear from these Figs. that, intensity of the currents decreases by increasing the number of cycles due to consumption of OH− in the solution. After an increase of the voltage to 3 V (Fig. 7c), the water electrolysis began to appear. If the voltage rises to 4 and 5 V the electrolysis of water will be further. Such intercalation and electrolysis behavior has been observed in previous study of the SDS graphite intercalation. It can be seen from Fig. 7(c, d and e) that the intensity of the oxidation peaks of water was decreased as a consequence of repeated potential scans because there was not enough solute OH− in the ethanol and trace water electrolytes, indicating that the amount of OH− and water was reduced. Moreover, by increasing of voltages and cycles the color of solution changes from light yellow to dark brown and it can show OH− hydrolysis intensity; it is important to tell that this hydrolysis can produce yields that will corrode the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS investigation of graphite corrosion in alkaline media
ce:section.ce:para:
In the process of graphite converting to CQD graphite corrosion was occurred. So, EIS is the usual method to assess how the corrosion process takes place. An EIS investigation was carried out at different potentials in order to evaluate the effect of various voltages on graphite rod and clearly apperceive CQD production (Figs. 8 and 9
). EIS measurements were performed with a 5 mV voltage magnitude in the 100 kHz–10 mHz frequency rang. Fig. 8 shows the Nyquist plots for graphite rod corrosion at different voltages from 0.1 to 0.9 V in alkaline and ethanol solution. The shape of plots changed at different potentials, reflecting different electrochemical processes occurring on the electrode surface. This Fig. indicates that as potential increases from 0.1 to 0.9 V the double layer capacitance (Cdl) decreases. This decrease may be due to the adsorption of the compounds on the graphite surface leading to a film formation. The experimental results were fitted using an appropriate equivalent circuit [37,38] depicted as an inset in Fig. 8. As it is obvious, the circuit consists of a parallel combination of a charge transfer resistance with a double layer capacitance in series with a Faradic capacitance (T), in addition to the solution resistance at high frequency extreme. Moreover, it is found that by increasing the potentials to 1, 2 and 3 V the electrolyte resistance (Re) is stable (Fig. 9). However, the higher applied potential has smaller charge/ion diffusion resistance (Rct) and smaller surface resistance (Rsf) than the smaller applied potential. On the other hand, single semicircle at the high frequency region and a straight line at the low frequency region in Fig. 9a, b, c indicate a mixed charge transfer and charge diffusion process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical behavior of ORR on CQD
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the electro-catalytic activity of CQD, the CVs in O2- or N2-saturated 0.5 M KOH solutions for GC and CQD/GC electrodes were measured at 50 mV−1. As shown in Fig. 10 a, no peak is seen in CV of bare GCE saturated by N2. For the GC and CQD/GC electrodes as shown in Fig. 10(b and c), however, markedly different result was observed. As shown distinct peaks corresponding to ORRs can be observed for GC and CQD/GC electrodes in O2 –saturated, in contrast to GC in N2-saturated in which have no clear redox peaks. To evaluate the long-term stability of CQD/GCE toward ORR in alkaline solution, the potential was continuously cycled in O2-saturated 0.5 M KOH for 3000 cycles. After the 3000 scans, the peak current slightly decreased (Fig. 10d).

Fig. 11 a shows polarization curves for three GDEs in a conventional three-electrode compartment cell. As shown in Fig. 12 no current was absorbed for ORR on bare carbon paper (CP). However, the current density on new GDE based on CQD/CP(GDE1) was half of 10 wt% PtC (E-TEK) paste on CP (GDE2). The electrode kinetics parameters of the GDE1 and GDE2 in low current density region were calculated to be 46 and 60 mV per decade. GDE1shows a lower Tafel slope which can be attributed to an enhanced electro-catalytic effect resulting from the presence of CQD in the catalyst layer. This displays the transfer of the first electron on both of these two electrocatalysts is the rate determining step under Temkin conditions for the adsorption of intermediates [39]. In the high current density region, the Tafel slops of GDE1and GDE2 were estimated to be 111 and 202 mV/decade. This result is assigned to a change in the mechanism of ORR from Temkin to Langmuir adsorption conditions when the current density increases [39].

Chronoamperometry is the usual method employed to estimate the permeability and stability of electroactive species at electrode surfaces. This method was used to determine the quantitative permeability of the GDE1 and GDE2 according to the Cottrell equation [40]. Fig. 12a shows typical I vs. t data obtained from the chronoamperometric experiments. Fig. 12b shows these data plotted as I versus t−1/2 plots. As can be inferred from Fig. 12b, the I-t−1/2 data were obtained in the time window between 0.4 and 1.6. Fig. 12b indicates the linear relationship for both GDEs. The oxygen permeability in the GDE containing CQD (GDE1) is very close to GDE2 with PtC (10 × 10−11 mol cm−2 s−1/2). This result is consistent with the ability of CQD to enhance the solubility and permeability of oxygen in the reaction layer due to its high porosity.

CQD has been obtained by green and fast electrochemical rout in the presence of NaOH/EtOH to produce GDEs. EIS of graphite in NaOH/EtOH solution shows the corrosion loop at low frequencies. The XRD pattern of the CQD contains a broad band in 2Tetha limits of approximately 22 and 29° (d = 0.39 nm). SEM and TEM investigations revealed rough and full of spherical nanoparticles of graphite. Raman has demonstrated low defect density in the CQD synthesis by electrochemical methods. Finally, CQD was painted on the surface of carbon paper and used as GDE in ORR. LSV curves show two Tafel slops and following different reaction kinetics. The oxygen permeability in the CQD gas diffusion layer is very close to commercial Pt/C catalyst. We concluded the CQD pasted on carbon paper is more suitable for applying in the GDEs in comparison with the standard Pt/C catalysts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the East Azarbaijan Science and Technology Park (EASTP).
